story:
“Don’t put your hands in the pockets!”

Jimmy’s sister said it like a rule, like something older kids just knew. He stared at the used coat he’d picked himself—blue denim, thick, soft fleece lining that felt like his grandma’s comforter, and so many zippered pockets with long chrome fobs that swung and flashed. He stood straighter in the mirror, proud and a little taller. “Why not?” he asked, teasing a fob between his fingers.

“Because that’s where people keep their wishes. And you’re not supposed to let the wishes out.”

Winter settled in deep and stayed. The blue denim coat with the thick, soft fleece lining and the many zippered pockets kept Jimmy warm on the mile-long walk to school and back. It shrugged off snow as he flew down hills on his sled, held his laughter in its lining when snowballs smacked and burst. It went with him to the woodpile, to the chores, to the cold breath of night.

One afternoon he lingered too long in the woods. Snow began, then thickened. In minutes the world turned white and wind erased every path. Night folded down without a seam. Jimmy couldn’t tell trees from shadows, couldn’t tell up from anywhere. He huddled under a fallen trunk, cupped his hands, and breathed into them. “I wish I hadn’t forgotten my gloves,” he whispered, aching. Without thinking, he unzipped the right pocket and sank his hand inside. Something soft. Something known. His wool gloves—warm and dry, as if they’d been waiting for him.

He blinked. “I wish it would stop snowing,” he said, tugging open the left pocket. The wind dropped like a curtain. The snow stilled.

His sister’s voice came back: That’s where people keep their wishes.

Jimmy smiled, the kind of smile that lights from the inside. He understood now. He pulled carefully at the zipper of the breast pocket. “I wish I wasn’t lost,” he called, loud enough for the trees to hear. The metal teeth parted, and the clouds did too. Moonlight spilled across the blanketed forest, silver and sure. He jumped up, laughed, and ran, the old blue denim coat with the thick, soft fleece lining and the many zippered pockets hugging him close as he aimed for home.

Branches clawed, brush grabbed, and in his hurry one long fob snagged. It pulled the large zippered bottom pocket open. Jimmy stumbled and fell into a drift, flailing, snow in his mouth, cold against his neck. The quiet cracked with a growl.

He looked up. A wolf loomed—huge, hungry, eyes like embers, teeth like icicles. Jimmy’s mind raced. What else had his sister said? You’re not supposed to let the wishes out.

Not every wish is kind. Some wishes are sharp. Some wishes growl.

The wolf crouched, all muscle and hunger. Jimmy lunged for the zipper, fingers clumsy in his gloves, trying to pull it closed, to tuck whatever had slipped free back where it belonged.

But it was too late.
